title: Doctor 'Procedural mesh registry' line silently lost when bootstrap aliases the registry to a local var
summary: engine/tools/doctor/project-doctor.ts:964 â€” `summarizeProceduralMeshes` discovers registered keys via the regex `/proceduralMeshRegistry\(\)\.register\(\s*["']([^"']+)["']/g`. This locks the scan to the literal call form `runtime.renderer.proceduralMeshRegistry().register("...", ...)`. S101's procbomber-bench bootstrap used exactly that form and the AGF-PROCMESH-DOCTOR-LINE acceptance â€” 'Procedural mesh registry: 1 declared key(s) [procbomber], 1 scene entity reference(s)' â€” was correct at S101 merge. S102 PROCBOMBER-MESH-TREE-V0 refactored the bootstrap to alias the registry once + register 6 part keys, the common readable pattern: `const procRegistry = runtime.renderer.proceduralMeshRegistry(); procRegistry.register("procbomber-torso", ...); ...` (5 more lines). The aliased form is correct runtime code but the regex never sees `proceduralMeshRegistry()` immediately adjacent to `.register(...)`, so `declaredKeys` collapses to `[]` and `formatProceduralMeshes` suppresses the whole section. Net effect: the doctor's procedural-mesh observability is silently gone the moment a project registers more than one key (the natural moment a developer aliases the helper). Scene-side static count (`sceneUsageCount`) is also 0 for procbomber-bench because the 19-entity bomber tree is spawned at runtime via `bomber-tree-spawner.ts`, not declared in `scenes/start.scene.json` â€” that's by design, but it means the section relies entirely on `declaredKeys` and the regex must be permissive enough to catch the aliased pattern. Not a regression in the runtime registry (proc meshes render fine â€” confirmed via `runtime.renderer.proceduralMeshRegistry()` returning the live keys when introspected from `window.__agf`), only in the doctor's static scan.
repro: From a fresh origin/main checkout (HEAD 4cde337 or later), run `npm run engine:doctor -- examples/procbomber-bench`.
Scroll the output looking for a 'Procedural mesh registry:' line. There is none.
Confirm the bootstrap genuinely registers 6 keys: `grep -n 'register("procbomber' examples/procbomber-bench/bootstrap.ts` â€” 6 hits (torso/head/upperArm/forearm/upperLeg/lowerLeg).

Confirm the static scan is the failure point: `node -e "const fs=require('node:fs'); const body=fs.readFileSync('examples/procbomber-bench/bootstrap.ts','utf8'); const re=/proceduralMeshRegistry\(\)\.register\(\s*[\"']([^\"']+)[\"']/g; let m,keys=[]; while((m=re.exec(body))) keys.push(m[1]); console.log('Regex caught:', keys);"` â€” prints `Regex caught: []`.

Confirm runtime is fine â€” boot `?project=procbomber-bench` in the browser, then `window.__agf.runtime.renderer.proceduralMeshRegistry().keys()` lists all 6 keys (registry is populated at runtime, only the offline scan is blind).
expected: When a project registers procedural mesh keys â€” whether via the inline `proceduralMeshRegistry().register(...)` form OR via the natural alias `const procRegistry = ...proceduralMeshRegistry(); procRegistry.register(...)` form â€” engine doctor reports `Procedural mesh registry: 6 declared key(s) [procbomber-torso, procbomber-head, procbomber-upperArm, procbomber-forearm, procbomber-upperLeg, procbomber-lowerLeg], 0 scene entity reference(s)` (or whatever counts apply). The per-key sub-list still appears for any keys that scenes reference but the project never registers (the existing âš  flag).
actual: Doctor output skips the 'Procedural mesh registry:' section entirely. The format function's suppression gate (`if (report.declaredKeys.length === 0 && report.sceneUsageCount === 0)`) fires because the static-scan regex doesn't catch the aliased `procRegistry.register(...)` form. Devs (and the dogfood doctor section that was supposed to surface this) get no signal that a project has procedural meshes at all.
